channel.title: Windows 11 Release notes
channel.description: Windows 11 update history and message center announcements

item.title: Furthering Windows as the trusted platform for development
item.description: New tools and AI-powered capabilities designed to help developers build faster, smarter, and more secure experiences are coming to Windows. These updates focus on making Windows more trustworthy, more productive, and better prepared for the next wave of AI innovation. Microsoft is also expanding the technologies and partnerships that help developers create modern apps that work across devices and cloud-connected experiences. Read the recap of Windows announcements at Microsoft Build to learn how this translates into better apps, stronger security, and more seamless experiences for you across work and everyday life.

item.title: Windows news you can use: May 2026 recap
item.description: In this month’s edition of Windows news you can use, we’re highlighting updates designed to streamline patching, improve Secure Boot visibility, and simplify cloud management across your Windows environment. Catch up on hotpatch updates now enabled by default for organizations using Windows Autopatch through Microsoft Intune or the Windows updates API, along with expanded hotpatch availability for Windows Server 2025 via Azure Arc at no additional cost. You’ll also find improved Secure Boot visibility with an updated Windows Autopatch report and a new Microsoft Defender assessment, as well as changes that make Windows Autopatch more accessible for eligible Microsoft 365 GCC customers.

item.title: The May 2026 Windows non-security preview update is now available
item.description: The May 2026 non-security preview update is now available for Windows 11, versions 26H1, 25H2, and 24H2. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows clause. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: May 26, 2026—KB5089573 (OS Builds 26200.8524 and 26100.8524) Preview
item.description: Windows 11 update: May 26, 2026—KB5089573 (OS Builds 26200.8524 and 26100.8524) Preview

item.title: Updated Secure Boot status report in Windows Autopatch
item.description: Windows Autopatch now delivers deeper, device-level insights into Secure Boot certificate status, trust configuration, and readiness. With new signals like confidence level, alerts, and detailed certificate visibility, you can more confidently move forward with broad updates and targeted remediations. Explore the latest updates to the Secure Boot stats report and learn how to gain more clarity into your Secure Boot rollout.

item.title: Windows Office Hours: May 21, 2026
item.description: If you are an IT admin with questions about managing and updating Windows, we want to help. Every third Thursday of the month, we host a live chat-based event on the Tech Community called Windows Office Hours. Members of the Windows, Microsoft Intune, Windows Autopilot, Windows Autopatch, and Windows 365 engineering teams will be standing by to answer your questions.

item.title: Tune in May 18 to ask Microsoft anything about Secure Boot
item.description: Secure Boot certificates start expiring in June of 2026. If you need guidance on rolling out updated certificates, post your questions in advance or in real time on the Tech Community for the May 18th AMA. Whether you’re actively deploying or just starting to plan, get clear, practical answers directly from Microsoft experts. Learn why these updates matter, how to identify impacted devices, and best practices for planning and rollout across your organization. Designed for both technical teams and decision makers, this ongoing series of AMAs is designed to help you move forward with confidence.

item.title: The May 2026 Windows security update is now available
item.description: The May 2026 security update is now available for all supported versions of Windows. We recommend that you install these updates promptly. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: Configuring firewall and proxies for smooth Windows updates
item.description: If you’re an IT admin, a new guide is here to help you troubleshoot connection issues with the Windows Update service. Some of these issues have to do with the embedded network security design. The key is in the configuration of your network endpoints for firewalls and proxies. Essentially, make sure to trust Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN) subdomains of the recommended DNS subdomains. Dive deeper at Configuring firewall and proxies for smooth Windows updates .

item.title: Upgrade to Windows Server 2025, now via Windows Update
item.description: Windows Server supports several methods for moving to a newer version, including in-place upgrade. This method installs a newer version of Windows Server over the existing one, keeping your settings, server roles, features, and data.

item.title: Windows news you can use: April 2026 edition
item.description: In this month’s edition of Windows news you can use, we’re highlighting a mix of product updates, practical guidance, and new reporting capabilities to support the ongoing management of your Windows environment. Catch up on a new Windows Autopatch report with enhanced risk visibility and compliance guidance, along with hardening changes to help reduce privilege escalation. You’ll also find new controls for removing preinstalled apps, improvements to Intune app inventory syncing, and expanded policy-based management features. Additionally, explore updates to Windows 365 monitoring and reporting, new provisioning options, and enhancements to Narrator with Copilot integration.

item.title: The April 2026 Windows non-security preview update is now available
item.description: The April 2026 non-security preview update is now available for Windows 11, versions 26H1, 25H2, and 24H2. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: Dynamically remove apps from managed Windows 11 devices
item.description: IT admins, you can now simplify Microsoft Store app management with dynamic removal on more devices across your enterprise. Use policy to remove any preinstalled MSIX/APPX app by referencing its Package Family Name (PFN). It’s available to Enterprise and Education devices running Windows 11, versions 25H2 and 24H2. Starting with the April 2026 Windows non-security update, you can use Group Policy or custom OMA-URI for mobile device management (MDM). Additional Microsoft Intune capabilities are coming later. Learn more at Dynamically remove apps from managed Windows 11 devices .

item.title: April 30, 2026—KB5083631 (OS Builds 26200.8328 and 26100.8328) Preview
item.description: Windows 11 update: April 30, 2026—KB5083631 (OS Builds 26200.8328 and 26100.8328) Preview

item.title: Assess Secure Boot certificate status with Microsoft Defender
item.description: Microsoft Defender now provides IT teams centralized visibility into Secure Boot 2023 certificate readiness across your device fleet. A new assessment categorizes your devices automatically as exposed, compliant, and not applicable. In the Defender portal, go to Exposure Management > Recommendations > Devices > Misconfigurations . For exposed devices, remediation guidance is directly available through the recommendation. Learn more in Preparing for Secure Boot 2023 with Microsoft Defender .

item.title: New IT admin policy: Remove Microsoft Copilot app
item.description: Improve user experience with a single Microsoft Copilot app to use on any given device. The new RemoveMicrosoftCopilotApp policy setting allows you to uninstall Copilot from devices in your organization in a non-disruptive way. It only applies to devices and users that meet the following conditions:

item.title: Ask Microsoft Anything about Secure Boot certificate updates
item.description: Have questions about updating Secure Boot certificates before they start expiring in June of 2026? Join one of our upcoming live Ask Microsoft Anything (AMA) events. Whether you’ve already in the process of deploying updates or are just getting started, this is your chance to get clear, practical answers directly from Microsoft. We can help explain why these updates are important and how to identify affected devices. We can help with tips on planning and rolling out updates across your organization. No matter your role or experience level, we’re here to help you get prepared with confidence. Add the April 23 Secure Boot AMA or May 18 Secure Boot AMA to your calendar.

item.title: Take action: Out-of-band updates released for Windows Server to address two issues
item.description: Microsoft has released out-of-band (OOB) updates today, April 19, 2026, to address issues introduced by the April 2026 Windows security update.

item.title: Interim guidance for Egypt DST changes 2026
item.description: For Windows devices in Egypt, a workaround is available for an issue affecting Daylight Saving Time (DST). Between April 24 and April 30, 2026, calendar events and meeting times in Egypt will appear off by one hour only for Windows Server 2016. This is due to a calendar edge case: a five-week April in 2026. Clocks will be correctly set to Egypt DST time zone on May 1, 2026, without any further intervention. If needed, a temporary workaround is available for the affected week. Learn more in Interim guidance for Egypt DST changes 2026 .

item.title: Windows news you can use: March 2026 recap
item.description: In this month’s edition of Windows news you can use, we’re highlighting an update from Pavan Davuluri on our continued commitment to Windows quality, followed by a roundup of recent announcements you may have missed. Catch up on the general availability of Windows Autopatch update readiness, hotpatch updates now turned on by default for eligible devices, and new RSAT support for Arm‑based Windows 11 PCs. You’ll also find updates on expanded regional availability for Windows 365 Frontline and a preview of what’s coming at the Windows Server Summit this May. See this month’s resources and recap at Windows news you can use: March 2026 .

item.title: Remote Desktop adds new safeguards for connections opened from RDP files
item.description: Starting with the April 2026 Windows security update, Remote Desktop adds new safeguards to help reduce phishing attacks that misuse Remote Desktop (.rdp) files. These attacks attempt to trick devices into connecting to attacker‑controlled systems and sharing local resources without clear visibility. To address this, a new security dialog appears before any connection is made, showing the remote computer address, publisher information (when available), and any requested access to local resources. All requested settings are disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled. A one‑time security warning also appears the first time an RDP file is opened. This change helps make potentially risky connections more visible and intentional, without affecting connections that are started manually in the Remote Desktop app.

item.title: Second deployment phase for Kerberos RC4 hardening begins with the April 2026 Windows security update
item.description: Windows updates released in April 2026 and later begin the second deployment phase of protections designed to address a Kerberos information disclosure vulnerability ( CVE‑2026‑20833 ). This second phase continues the shift away from legacy encryption types such as RC4 by moving toward stronger default ticket behavior. After installing the April 2026 update, domain controllers default to supporting Advanced Encryption Standard (AES‑SHA1) encrypted tickets for accounts that do not have an explicit Kerberos encryption type configuration.

item.title: The April 2026 Windows security update is now available
item.description: The April 2026 security update is now available for all supported versions of Windows. We recommend that you install these updates promptly. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: Windows Deployment Services (WDS): Hands-free deployment hardening (Phase 2)
item.description: As announced in January 2026, the Unattend.xml file used in hands‑free deployment poses a vulnerability when transmitted over an unauthenticated RPC channel. Beginning with the April 2026 security update, the second phase of hardening changes for CVE-2026-0386 is now in effect. These changes make hands‑free deployment disabled by default to enforce secure behavior.

item.title: Windows Office Hours: April 16, 2026
item.description: If you are an IT admin with questions about managing and updating Windows, we want to help. Every third Thursday of the month, we host a live chat-based event on the Tech Community called Windows Office Hours. Members of the Windows, Microsoft Intune, Windows Autopilot, Windows Autopatch, and Windows 365 engineering teams will be standing by to answer your questions.

item.title: Hardening administrative actions: Windows imaging, cloning, and auth workflows
item.description: Administrative actions are undergoing hardening changes that might require operational change to support your organization’s security posture. With the August 2025 Windows non-security update, devices were hardened against unauthorized attempts to bypass loopback detection. However, if you’ve cloned machines without Sysprep, you might see Kerberos and NTLM authentication failures. This is by design. The recommended solution is to rebuild affected devices using supported imaging methods. A temporary workaround is also available. Learn more at Hardening administrative actions: What IT pros need to know .

item.title: Extended Security Updates (ESU) available for Windows 10 Enterprise LTSB 2016
item.description: If your organization relies on Windows 10 Enterprise LTSB 2016, you know it’s nearing end of support on October 13, 2026. While the long-term solution is upgrading to a newer LTSC release, another temporary solution is also now available. Extended Security Updates (ESU) are now available for purchase through Volume Licensing or a Microsoft Cloud Solution Provider (CSP). Learn more about this transition at Plan for Windows Server 2016 and Windows 10 2016 LTSB end of support .

item.title: ESR management is moving to Windows Backup for Organizations
item.description: If your IT department relies on Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) to synchronize user settings, plan for change. Starting May 2026, ESR management is moving to Windows Backup for Organizations. Previously, IT admins were able to access it from the Microsoft Entra portal. This old option will be still available until the end of June 2026. Please start accessing the new default experience through Windows Backup for Organizations policies . Learn more here .

item.title: Replacement of the Microsoft SaRA command-line utility in Windows
item.description: If you’re an IT admin, migrate away from the Microsoft Support and Recovery Assistant utility (SaRA) . This command-line utility is deprecated to help secure and harden your environment. The Get Help command-line tool has similar capabilities and is recommended as a replacement for the SaRA utility. You’ll need to download it and use GetHelpCmd.exe to run your scenarios. Learn more at Microsoft Support and Recovery Assistant (SaRA) command-line utility removal from Windows.

item.title: Secure Boot certificate update status in the Windows Security app
item.description: Windows Pro and Home users can now see the status of Secure Boot certificate updates in the Windows Security app. As the 2011 certificates are approaching expiration in 2026, updated 2023 certificates are being delivered automatically through Windows Update. In April 2026, in Device security > Secure Boot , you’ll see a green, yellow, or red badge indicating if you must take action. In May 2026, you’ll also see notifications outside the app, such as system alerts. Learn more about these status and action notices as well as how to dismiss warnings . This experience is disabled by default on IT-managed devices, but can be enabled.

item.title: Take Action: Out-of-band update to address an issue affecting installation of the March 2026 non-security preview update
item.description: Microsoft has identified an issue affecting installation of the March 2026 Windows non-security preview update on some devices running Windows 11, versions 25H2 and 24H2. While installing the update, the following error might occur: “Some update files are missing or have problems. We’ll try to download the update again later. Error code: (0x80073712)”

item.title: March 31, 2026—KB5086672 (OS Builds 26200.8117 and 26100.8117) Out-of-band
item.description: Windows 11 update: March 31, 2026—KB5086672 (OS Builds 26200.8117 and 26100.8117) Out-of-band

item.title: New resources to help organizations prepare for Secure Boot certificate expirations
item.description: Secure Boot certificates begin expiring in June 2026, and IT admins should take action now to help ensure devices remain secure and compliant. Timely deployment of updated certificates is essential to preserve device startup integrity and avoid servicing (i.e., updates) disruptions.

item.title: (Updated) The March 2026 Windows non-security preview update is now available
item.description: (Updated 3-31-2026: The March 2026 Windows non-security preview update has been replaced with out-of-band update KB5086672 for Windows 11, versions 25H2 and 24H2. The Windows 11 links in this message have been updated to reflect this change.)

item.title: March 26, 2026—KB5079391 (OS Builds 26200.8116 and 26100.8116) Preview
item.description: Windows 11 update: March 26, 2026—KB5079391 (OS Builds 26200.8116 and 26100.8116) Preview

item.title: Take Action: Out-of-band update released to address an issue signing in to apps with a Microsoft account
item.description: Microsoft has identified an issue affecting signing in to apps using a Microsoft account . After installing the March 2026 Windows security update, some users might be unable to sign in to Microsoft apps and services. During sign‑in, users might see a “no internet” error even when the device has a working internet connection. This issue affects only Microsoft account sign‑in scenarios; organizations using Microsoft Entra ID for app authentication are not affected.

item.title: March 21, 2026—KB5085516 (OS Builds 26200.8039 and 26100.8039) Out-of-band
item.description: Windows 11 update: March 21, 2026—KB5085516 (OS Builds 26200.8039 and 26100.8039) Out-of-band

item.title: Out-of-band hotpatch update released to address an issue affecting Bluetooth device visibility
item.description: Microsoft has identified an issue affecting hotpatch-enabled Enterprise editions of Windows where Bluetooth devices may not appear on the Bluetooth & devices page in Windows Settings or in Quick Settings , even though the devices are connected and functioning as expected. In addition, users might be unable to add Bluetooth devices because available devices do not appear in the connection list.

item.title: Out-of-band update released for Windows Enterprise client devices running hotpatch updates
item.description: Microsoft has identified a security issue in the Windows Routing and Remote Access Service (RRAS) management tool that could allow remote code execution when connecting to a malicious server. This issue only applies to a limited set of scenarios involving Enterprise client devices running hotpatch updates and being used for remote server management.

item.title: Windows Office Hours: March 19, 2026
item.description: If you are an IT admin with questions about managing and updating Windows, we want to help. Every third Thursday of the month, we host a live chat-based event on the Tech Community called Windows Office Hours. Members of the Windows, Microsoft Intune, Windows Autopilot, Windows Autopatch, and Windows 365 engineering teams will be standing by to answer your questions. We also have experts from FastTrack, the Customer Acceleration Team, and Microsoft public sector teams.

item.title: RSAT capabilities arrive on Arm-based Windows 11 PCs
item.description: You can now use familiar Remote Server Administration Tools (RSAT) on Arm-based Windows 11 devices. With the February 2026 non-security preview update, five of the most widely used RSAT components are available for everyday system administrator tasks. Add users to Active Directory, edit GPOs, configure NDS and DHCP servers, and more. Learn why RSAT on Arm64 matters, how it’s available on different versions of Windows 11, and how to enable it .

item.title: The March 2026 Windows security update is now available
item.description: The March 2026 security update is now available for all supported versions of Windows. We recommend that you install these updates promptly. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: Windows Autopatch is enabling hotpatch updates by default
item.description: Starting with the May 2026 Windows security update, Windows Autopatch is enabling hotpatch security updates by default because they are the quickest way to get secure. This change in default behavior will impact all eligible Microsoft Intune devices. Additional IT controls are coming in April. Read the announcement in Securing devices faster with hotpatch updates on by default .

item.title: Upcoming events to help with Secure Boot updates
item.description: Are you responsible for updating Secure Boot certificates across your organization? They start expiring in June 2026. Tune in next week for best practices and answers to your questions in real time. First up, on March 9 at Microsoft Technical Takeoff is Secure Boot certificate updates explained . This is a fast-paced walkthrough of essential guidance - including how to test firmware, monitor device readiness, deploy updated certificates, and diagnose issues. Have questions? Post them early and often for the March 12 Secure Boot AMA . Engineering teams will be answering on camera and in the comments.

item.title: Windows news you can use: February 2026 recap
item.description: In this month’s edition of Windows news you can use, we’re helping you catch up on upcoming events, new resources to help with Secure Boot certificate updates, and more. Explore the latest capabilities in Windows Backup for Organizations. Learn how Windows 365 Boot provide a differentiated experience for Windows 365 Reserve. If you’re an IT admin looking for what’s new in Windows device management, security, and cloud experiences for the enterprise, this is a good place to start.

item.title: Prepare devices for successful updates with Windows Autopatch update readiness
item.description: Windows Autopatch update readiness helps IT admins proactively identify and resolve issues that can block Windows updates before deployment. It provides visibility into device readiness across the Microsoft Intune-managed Windows fleet and surfaces actionable insights to reduce update failures and manual troubleshooting. By shifting update management from reactive to proactive, it helps improve update success rates and strengthen overall fleet reliability.

item.title: New Secure Boot update resources for Azure Virtual Desktop, Windows 365, and Microsoft Intune
item.description: New documentation is now available to help IT administrators prepare for Secure Boot certificate updates and manage update readiness across virtual and cloud‑managed Windows environments. This documentation includes Secure Boot certificate updates for Azure Virtual Desktop , Windows 365 Cloud PCs , and monitoring Secure Boot certificate status using Microsoft Intune remediations , providing visibility into readiness across managed devices. Together, these resources help ensure virtual and cloud‑managed Windows environments remain secure and continue to receive updates after June 2026.

item.title: Windows first sign-in restore experience now available
item.description: Windows first sign-in restore experience is now generally available as part of Windows Backup for Organizations . Users signing in for the first time on an eligible device will see a restore experience at first sign-in, even if it was missed the first time the device was turned on ( Windows out of box experience ). This allows users to restore Windows settings, restore their Microsoft Store app list, or choose to configure the device as new. This feature extends restore capabilities beyond Microsoft Entra-joined devices to support hybrid-managed environments, improving readiness for new or reimaged Windows 11 devices.

item.title: The February 2026 Windows non-security preview update is now available
item.description: The February 2026 non-security preview update is now available for Windows 11, versions 26H1, 25H2, and 24H2. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: February 24, 2026—KB5077241 (OS Builds 26200.7922 and 26100.7922) Preview
item.description: Windows 11 update: February 24, 2026—KB5077241 (OS Builds 26200.7922 and 26100.7922) Preview

item.title: Windows Server Secure Boot playbook for certificates expiring in 2026
item.description: Learn about tools and options available to organizations to update Secure Boot certificates on Windows Server. Certificates begin expiring in June 2026. You must update them before that date to help keep your security posture. Many recent platforms already include the supported 2023 certificates in firmware. However, for the ones that need to be updated, you need to manage this process manually. Get started today with the recommended approach in Windows Server Secure Boot playbook for certificates expiring in 2026 .

item.title: Personalize your Message center feed
item.description: The Message center in the Microsoft 365 admin center includes personalization features that help IT admins focus on Windows updates and reduce information overload. By adjusting service preferences, filters, and columns, IT admins can prioritize Windows feature updates, security and out‑of‑band updates, hardening changes, and lifecycle announcements most relevant to their environments.

item.title: Coming soon to organizations: Customize the Start menu with updated policies
item.description: The new Start menu is gradually rolling out to general audiences and will be available to organizations in the second quarter of the calendar year. Two policies allow IT admins to further customize the Start menu: HideCategoryView and ConfigureStartPins. Learn more about them at Start Policy CSP and Start policy settings .

item.title: Windows Office Hours: February 19, 2026
item.description: If you are an IT admin with questions about managing and updating Windows, we want to help. Every third Thursday of the month, we host a live chat-based event on the Tech Community called Windows Office Hours. Members of the Windows, Microsoft Intune, Windows Autopilot, Windows Autopatch, and Windows 365 engineering teams will be standing by to answer your questions. We also have experts from FastTrack, the Customer Acceleration Team, and Microsoft public sector teams.

item.title: Refreshing the root of trust: industry collaboration on Secure Boot certificate updates
item.description: Secure Boot is a foundational Windows security feature that runs at startup, before Windows load, and helps ensure that only trusted, digitally signed software can execute. After more than 15 years of continuous service, the original Secure Boot certificates are reaching the end of their planned lifecycle and begin expiring in late June 2026.

item.title: The February 2026 Windows security update is now available
item.description: The February 2026 security update is now available for all supported versions of Windows. We recommend that you install these updates promptly. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: (Updated) Resolved: Power outage affecting Microsoft Store app installs and Windows update delivery
item.description: 11:30 am PT update: Microsoft Store and Windows Update services have been restored. Some IT administrators may continue to experience residual latency affecting Windows Server update operations, including the Windows Server sync API. This Server‑specific issue is being addressed separately. If you experience this latency, please contact Microsoft Support for business .

item.title: Advancing Windows security: Disabling NTLM by default
item.description: Windows is moving toward a more secure authentication model by phasing out New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) in favor of stronger, Kerberos‑based alternatives. This transition is taking a three-phased approach, leading toward disabling NTLM by default in upcoming Windows releases. With each phase come new capabilities so that your organization has the tools, visibility, and compatibility support needed. Learn more at Advancing Windows security: Disabling NTLM by default.

item.title: Windows news you can use: January 2026 recap
item.description: In this month’s edition of Windows news you can use, we’re helping you catch up on important changes and evolving standards that will help strengthen the security of the Windows ecosystem across client, cloud, and server. Learn about new tools to help with Secure Boot certificate updates and a new command-line tool for Windows app developers. Explore features and enhancements that will start gradually rolling out in February, which include improvements to Cross-Device Resume, Narrator, Voice Access, MIDI support, and the Settings home page.

item.title: Skill up on Windows at Technical Takeoff
item.description: If you’re looking for prescriptive guidance help you confidently configure, manage, and support Windows in your organization, mark your calendars for Microsoft Technical Takeoff 2026. Taking place on the first four Mondays in March, this free event offers technical deep dives, AMAs, and feedback sessions—all supported by live Q&A with the teams behind the features. See what’s in store for Windows or browse the full agenda .

item.title: Secure Boot status report in Windows Autopatch
item.description: If you’re an IT admin using Windows Autopatch, a new report is now available to monitor the status of Secure Boot across your organization. As the 2011 Secure Boot certificates start expiring in June 2026 , this report helps you start by identifying devices that have Secure Boot enabled. Out of those, see which devices are fully up to date and which ones still need updated certificates. Learn more and get started with this report at Secure Boot status report in Windows Autopatch .

item.title: The January 2026 Windows non-security preview update is now available
item.description: The January 2026 non-security preview update is now available for Windows 11, versions 25H2 and 24H2. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: January 29, 2026—KB5074105 (OS Builds 26200.7705 and 26100.7705) Preview
item.description: Windows 11 update: January 29, 2026—KB5074105 (OS Builds 26200.7705 and 26100.7705) Preview

item.title: Take Action: Out-of-band update to address cloud‑backed storage application issues
item.description: Microsoft released today a resolution for an issue observed after installing the January 2026 Windows security update. This issue may cause applications that open or save files stored in cloud‑backed locations to become unresponsive or display errors. Some installations of Outlook may also become unresponsive and fail to open when PST files are stored in cloud‑backed storage such as OneDrive.

item.title: January 24, 2026—KB5078127 (OS Builds 26200.7628 and 26100.7628) Out-of-band
item.description: Windows 11 update: January 24, 2026—KB5078127 (OS Builds 26200.7628 and 26100.7628) Out-of-band

item.title: Ask Microsoft Anything: Secure Boot - February 5
item.description: Save the date and tune in live, February 5 at 8:00 AM PST, and Ask Microsoft Anything (AMA) about updating Secure Boot certificates on your Windows devices before they start expiring in June 2026. If you've already bookmarked the Secure Boot playbook , but need more details or have a specific question, this is a great opportunity to get the answers you need to prepare for this milestone. No question is too big or too small. Experts will be on camera and in the chat to help you with update scenarios, inventorying your estate, formulating the right deployment plan for your organization, and more. Visit the event page , then select Add to Calendar. Can’t attend at that time? Post your questions early then catch up on demand.

item.title: Take Action: Out-of-band update to address issues observed with the January 2026 Windows security update
item.description: Microsoft has identified issues upon installing the January 2026 Windows security update. To address these issues, an out-of-band (OOB) update was released today, January 17, 2026.

item.title: January 17, 2026—KB5077744 (OS Builds 26200.7627 and 26100.7627) Out-of-band
item.description: Windows 11 update: January 17, 2026—KB5077744 (OS Builds 26200.7627 and 26100.7627) Out-of-band

item.title: Windows Backup for Organizations expands to first sign-in restore
item.description: Preview the expanded Windows Backup for Organizations experience! In early 2026, if users miss the restore option during the out-of-box experience (OOBE) by accident or due to a technical issue, they can still restore at first sign-in. Windows Backup for Organizations helps preserve Windows settings, the list of installed Microsoft Store apps, and the Start menu pins. And now, even more devices can take advantage of this capability, including Microsoft Entra hybrid joined, multi-user setups, and Windows 365 Cloud PCs. Sign up for the private preview now through February 13, 2026.

item.title: The January 2026 Windows security update is now available
item.description: The January 2026 security update is now available for all supported versions of Windows. We recommend that you install these updates promptly. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: Hardening changes coming to Common Log File System (CLFS) authentication
item.description: Windows 11, version 25H2 and Windows Server 2025 updates released on or after October 28, 2025 include a new hardening authentication mitigation for the Common Log File System (CLFS) driver. This change adds a hash-based message authentication code (HMAC) to the underlying files of a CLFS logfile. Authentication codes are generated by combining file data with a system-unique cryptographic key stored in the registry, accessible only to administrators and SYSTEM accounts. This mechanism allows CLFS to verify file integrity before parsing internal structures; if the integrity check fails, the logfile will not open.

item.title: Windows Office Hours: January 15, 2026
item.description: If you are an IT admin with questions about managing and updating Windows, we want to help. Every third Thursday of the month, we host a live chat-based event on the Tech Community called Windows Office Hours. Members of the Windows, Microsoft Intune, Windows Autopilot, Windows Autopatch, and Windows 365 engineering teams will be standing by to answer your questions. We also have experts from FastTrack, the Customer Acceleration Team, and Microsoft public sector teams.

item.title: Get started with December 2025 improvements in Windows 11
item.description: Start using the newest Windows 11 capabilities with helpful tips and actionable steps summarized in one place. Discover IT resources to prepare for Secure Boot certificate update, track and remediate CVEs in Windows Autopatch, opt in to native NVMe support in Windows Server 2025, and much more to help keep your organization protected and productive.

item.title: Skill up on Windows and AI from our updated library of skilling snacks
item.description: Looking to grow in practical knowledge of Windows and AI? Get started with:

item.title: Take Action: Out-of-band update to address MSMQ service issues leading to queue operations and resource-related errors
item.description: Microsoft has identified an issue affecting Message Queuing (MSMQ) functionality in some enterprise environments after installing the December 2025 Windows security update (released December 9, 2025). Individuals using Windows Home or Pro editions on personal devices are very unlikely to experience this issue, as it primarily affects enterprise or managed IT environments.

item.title: Windows Office Hours: December 18, 2025
item.description: If you are an IT admin with questions about managing and updating Windows, we want to help. Every third Thursday of the month, we host a live chat-based event on the Tech Community called Windows Office Hours. Members of the Windows, Microsoft Intune, Windows Autopilot, Windows Autopatch, and Windows 365 engineering teams will be standing by to answer your questions. We also have experts from FastTrack, the Customer Acceleration Team, and Microsoft public sector teams.

item.title: Latest on Windows quality updates out of the box – now disabled by default
item.description: Starting with the January 2026 security update, the AllowOOBEUpdates CSP policy will be available to IT admins and disabled by default. It shows up as a new setting on the Windows Autopilot Enrollment Status Page (ESP). This policy allows you to install the latest Windows quality updates during the out-of-box experience (OOBE) on eligible devices. Devices must be Microsoft Entra joined or Microsoft Entra hybrid joined and running Windows 11, version 22H2 or later. Read the updated announcement in Get ready for Windows quality updates out of the box as well as its accompanying documentation .

item.title: The December 2025 Windows security update is now available
item.description: The December 2025 security update is now available for all supported versions of Windows. We recommend that you install these updates promptly. Information about the contents of this update is available from the release notes, which are accessible from the Windows 11 update history page. For instructions on how to install this update on your home device, check the Update Windows article. To learn more about the different types of monthly quality updates, see Windows monthly updates explained . To be informed about the latest updates and releases, follow us on X @WindowsUpdate .

item.title: How to use Microsoft Intune to update expiring Secure Boot certificates
item.description: If you’re an IT admin, you can now deploy, manage, and monitor Secure Boot certificate updates using Microsoft Intune. This method represents an alternative to setting registry keys and using Group Policy. You can now use Intune to trigger deployment on all domain-joined Windows clients, opt out of high-confidence buckets, and opt in to Microsoft managing these updates.

item.title: Secure Boot AMA: Ask Microsoft Anything on December 10
item.description: Join us December 10, 2025, at 8:00 AM PST for a live Ask Microsoft Anything (AMA) session focused on updating Secure Boot certificates on your Windows devices before they expire in June 2026. This event gives IT admins the chance to ask questions and get expert guidance on Secure Boot configuration, update scenarios, inventorying and preparing your environment, and formulating the right deployment plan for your organization. Members of the Windows team will be available to answer your questions, share tips, and provide insights to help you prepare devices for Secure Boot certificate deployment.

item.title: New Windows Autopatch reports on CVEs
item.description: A new Windows Autopatch report empowers your organization to prioritize update deployment, demonstrate compliance, and maintain a robust security posture. The Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs) report joins your other Windows quality update reports right in the Microsoft Intune admin center. Use it to view Windows CVEs addressed by recent quality updates and follow links to remediation documentation and device-level vulnerability status. Get started at New Windows Autopatch reports on CVEs .
